textify(ec||(ec=(0,re.Z)([" You walk over to the short, light-blue android as he is readjusting the pack strapped around his waist. The badge on his standard uniform reads ‘Terence’. Guess that would be his name. Glowing blue eyes dart to you from within their dark, empty uni-socket, briefly flaring bright that someone approaches his station. Conspicuously, he scans you in full once you are close, swiftly followed by a twirling finger, as if to announce something you obviously lack. “Excuse me, cadet,” the android peacekeeper politely stops you from moving any closer with his soft voice. “Where is your uniform? I do not see one on your person. At this current time, all at-entry officers must be in uniform to proceed past this checkpoint. If you do not have one, you will have to notify your supervisor immediately.” You tell the android that he is mistaken, that you are a visitor, not a member of any armed force. You get your codex, popping out a copy of your ID show him. “O-oh! You are [pc.mr] [pc.fullName]! I am very sorry for the misunderstanding,” the android expresses sheepishly, subtly shrinking himself, cap tilted over his eyes. “There has been an influx of peacekeepers recently. You sure appeared to be another one.” Really? You look down at your "," and back up at him. Shrugging, you pardon the light-blue officer for his mistake. It’s not exactly a cure for his meek embarrassment, but he at least picks chin up when you ask if he will let you through now. “Uuuh, yes. Of course you may,” he answers, dropping his strange stupor. After shuffling backward, he butts up against a new-looking, tall, mechanical arch. The android starts, then turns around to pat it, saying, “But firstly, if you would, please allow me to inspect your possessions, [pc.mr].” You nod and follow procedures like everyone else, emptying your pockets, shimmying off your pack, and placing your stuff bin to pass through a compact conveyor of mysteries. No noisy beeps. Good. Your equipment is searched by unnaturally sleek-looking hands; not too invasive, not too neglectful, just ten simple seconds of dainty sifting and he’s done. Right as you walk away, the helpful, albeit blundering android murmurs, timidly extending an arm in a cute attempt to grabbing your attention again, likely to formally introduce himself — and also complete some inner algorithm while it is still available. “O-oh, and I almost forgot to introduce myself,” he awkwardly mentions. Duly, he draws up, arms pressed down, only to have them bent outward by the pronounced swell of his hips. “I am a praetor model-number two, policing service android. Unit number six-two-four-three-two. But, if you could please kindly call me Terence. Or Officer Terence is fine too. My emotional feedback system would process my appreciation if you did so.” Smiling at the little display of honor, you greet Officer Terence by his given name. The most basic trade of respect flusters the short, P-2 robot, sinking his head down. He snaps to a corner, holding a hand to his mouth, though you barely conceal the big grin spreading his cheeks. If he somehow had blood in his circuits, he’d be bluing from chin to cheeks and tinting the inky-black socket surrounding his oculars. Peeking at you, Terence murmurs, “You can come by my station if you ever want a quick pass on inspection. I am actually reserved for officials and corporate affiliates. textify(ac||(ac=(0,re.Z)([" You cover a cough — a loud, hoarse one — and respectfully point out that he’s made a grave mistake. One might even say a mistake so severe it could be punishable by the highest courts in the galaxy. “Excuse me. Terence. Don’t you think there’s a better way for me to check out your structural integrity?” The android tilts his noggin in confusion, nervously replying, “Um. I do not understand, [pc.mr] Steele. You can feel the artificial dermis from my arms like anywhere else on my body. What other method is there that will provide more?” To answer his question, you silently scoot up and lean into Terence’s side, your pointing finger in range of its target: his substantial rear end. His furrowed stare follows your pointing, only for his glowy eyes to flicker in realization. “O-oh!” he blurts, darting from side to side, flicking back to you before craning his head back to examine the shapely pair of globes pulling the rear fabric of his pants thin. “I um... yes... I understand now. I guess examining my bottom would yield a better result. U-uuuh. Be my guest, [pc.mr] [pc.name]. If you could just be discreet about it, plea-.” Your [pc.ears] heard only his go-ahead, hands diving deep into his form-fitting trousers, rubbing and squeezing away at an ultraslick, ultrasoft surface of synthetic buttflesh. The robotic peacekeeper snaps his mouth open and utters a muted exclamation, startled at the blatant enthusiasm you’re showing his two juicy specs. Terence holds on to your biceps for balance, tugging you both into a concealed niche, frantic about avoiding the eyes of passersby who thought they peeked a handsy [pc.raceShort] copping a feel of his backside. The P-2’s flesh is very yielding... very yielding indeed. Your fingers — hell, your hands! - sink right into these blue cheeks the second you get settled, consuming your meager gropes so easily that you might be the one who jumped in wholly unprepared. The depths existing within this bottom-heavy bot are cutting edge. This advanced sophistication keeps your hungry grip under his pants. While you aren’t sure at first if he feels the same heightened sensation you would if someone were to feel up your behind, rolling and mashing your asscheeks together, the faint “mh” and soft “oh” whispered in your [pc.ear] certainly seem to suggest that he’s more than responsive to your touch. You move a little closer for a good view of the clothed booty wobbling in your outspread grip. Peeking down, you can hardly see any defining indents of your hands beneath his pants. It’s just two ruffled spots centered atop their own hemispherical mountains, occasionally wobbling and quaking. After a few more minutes spent gripping and kneading — grinning with satisfaction all the while — you confirm with Terence that he’s certainly telling the truth about his model’s supple superiority. You pull yourself out of his snug pants, but not before lightly smacking that fine tush. Your palms bounce off his cheeks with a jiggle that promptly sends his body wiggling, undoing any creases you may have made. The P-2 pats himself down, ensuring every fabric-straining curve filling up his pants is flush and neatly packed. Terence pulls up his forgotten shirt sleeves, mumbling. “Thorough, though that hardly lets one fully realize the makeup of my design. After all, you were only examining my exterior.” If rubbing your hands together wasn’t telling enough, you reply with a wicked, “Let me back there again. I’ll squeeze your handfuls harder this time.” As you say it, Terence’s shoulders fly up to his neck, balled hands to his mouth after noticing something — or clearly someone — staring from behind you. The government android zips straight to his post, eyes bright as flashlights. “B-but... butno... it is okay. This will do, [pc.mr] Steele. Butt my butt surely has sufficed in forming a conclusion. I am happy to be of help to you, [pc.mf|sir|ma’am].” You twist your head, surprised to see only stacked crates and windows to deep space. Nothing to jolt his circuits like that. What a weird fella. "])))

textify(rc||(rc=(0,re.Z)([" Terence continues the conversation. “And unlike the former C-series, we have unique V.I. sentience algorithms made up of nearly half a million lines of code, where each line generates one of a thousand different responses to all manner of stimuli. It is not nearly as advanced as the mainframe of a shipyard supercomputer, but is a predominant component keeping me and my sibling androids from supersedure for over a century.” It is funny the way Terence describes his point. At most, these P-2s operate on rudimentary social intelligence; though you won’t say that to his face. You will mention that if the UGC is producing their googly-eyed service robots en masse, it would only make sense to skim time and money by settling on quantity over quality. Terence quirks his lips at your statement. “I would reckon the P-2s favor quality. The audio receptors you see from the sides of my head, for example. They help me pick up all sorts of frequencies. Oh, um... Yeah! As you may know, we policing service androids are the top-of-the-line in holding our post with minimum wear and tear. I am speaking literally. I can stand in this exact position or sit in a chair for days, weeks, months on end without requiring a maintenance and performance check. This here makes such a feat possible.” Terence places both hands on his hips, gliding up and down to demonstrate the superb broadness he calls his support. Too novice or too shortsighted to notice, he is pulling more and more wandering eyes to himself, smacking perturbed grins on the faces as far as thirty feet away. “The increased silicone concentrations in my lower section reduces stress to the joints and circuits. It enables me to lean like you are, but longer and without any complications.” “Mm right. Quite the quality assets you got there,” you mumble through rolled-up lips, flicking a cursory glance at two well-dressed ladies openly giggling a short distance away. The pair are deliberately pointing their amusement at the titanic tooshie your oblivious friend is outlining, boosting the eye count of others in the process. Glowing an innocent smile, Terence stops embarrassing himself any further. You have to give the dude some slack; he’s a robot, so of course his battery-powered brain is missing some basic social awareness. "])))

textify(uc||(uc=(0,re.Z)([" You pull away from a rowdy bunch stampeding through the checkpoint, then ask, “Is there any variation among your robotic circle? Unit A designed for this, Units B made to do those, anything like that?” “Um. Not really.” Terence answers, scratching his lower cheek. “I mean... not around here, that is. The data I was logged with is limited in this regard. The first-made AP-UI android is written on the roster. But they are assigned elsewhere instead of this command post. So, yes, P-2 units mostly securing areas, maintain order, and bring color to the eyes of our citizens. Uum, sorry... That was not my intended script. I am not saying we are the best asset the United Galactic Confederacy needs in an all-purpose purview. True as it may be, spending all resources in one series is simply impractical. Even then, [pc.mr] Steele, we have our uses; such uses have come especially handy for these current conditions.” “Interesting,” you nod. “So only blue and pink bots is what I’m likely to find in your line?” “N-no,” the nervous android stutters. “I mean... yes. My sibling constructs and I can share the same... peculiarity. Bu-but we have a whole lot of things that make each of us different or unique. It is at the core of our programming. The day we were brought in to serve.” He plants a hand on his soft chest, clutching meaningfully to whatever substitute a beating heart. “I am Terence. I possess my own niche that molded me as an individual construct. I like this... cap. This cap was rewarded to me for perfect attendance, and this... waist-line compartment I store my tools and writing utensils in.” Terence catches you off guard when he abruptly springs an arm up, pointing to a specific direction without actually looking there himself. There, you see a pair of uniformed P-2s, like him, conversing with one another near a water fountain. One, a pink-toned lass, relaxes her shoulders and wide hips on the wall, punked out with a skull keychain pierced in her right audio receptor, followed by another skull glittered over her right cheek and cheap-looking but vibrant watch worn on her left wrist. The other one has the same light-blue skin as your bashful compadre here. Only, his outer attire puts him forever ready to hit the gym after a long day of work, fastened with elastic, green bands over his head and wrists. The pink P-2 actively listens to her light-blue brother, nodding her head as he amuses her on a subject unknown to you. It seems dubious. He chirps about something while making revealing hand gestures. Like a mime, he grips a physical rod of air, stroking up and down, meticulously kneading here and there on specific spots. Cute. “Over there are two other service units like me. ‘Deedee’ two-nine-nine-four-two-one and old ‘Paiso’ five-three-four-four. Deedee enjoys engrossing herself in visual novels during her spare time. I always see this strange, two-faced creature popping on her holo when I walk in on her. Paiso frequents the exercise blocks whenever he can assist anyone in their powerlifting. He says he provides refreshments or, at times, administers stress relief. He is a generous servant we all look up to.” Those two do stand out among most. “Mhm. And there is more you should know,” Terence elaborates, blue eyes flaring up. “There exists my bigger and stronger cousins, the combat androids. While us service units are allocated to safer and simpler tasks, combat units like ‘Fazon’ four-zero-two-zero or ‘Celski’ one-zero-six-five-three take to the fight for justice and the greater good. Me, I could never simulate myself charging on the front lines. Calculating the thousands of variables to my probable deletion, I uum... I would overload... my systems practically force a shutdown at the thought.” You hum, having suspected much the same. “So the combat ones have a mind of their own, maybe enough for a social circle?” “At production, all P-2 combat units are given as many ego scripts as me.” Terence’s weak smile weakens more with uncertainty. “Actually, um... possibly more scripts. Possibly with the increasing number of ‘reconditioned’ P-2s lately. Often, most of them are combat units. You may not know what ‘reconditioning’ means. You should not be concerned by this, [pc.mr] Steele, but understand me when I tell you it is a hijacking that inflicts very, very weird processes on us androids. Very weird... processes... U-uuuh. I think we are done here.” Just like that, Terence folds, cutting his chatter off to stand idle, zoned out from seemingly everything. He is his own person, after all. "])))

textify(hc||(hc=(0,re.Z)([" “Tell me about yourself, Terence,” you ask, your gentle tone inviting the introverted bot-boy. Terence squirms in silence, flustered by an apparently uncommon prospect of summarizing his life in words. He eventually cracks after you nudge his shoulder a few times. “I do not have much to tell you, [pc.mr] Steele,” he nervously admits, rubbing his left arm. “For most of my operation, I worked for the United Galactic Confederacy — those who built me for precisely such a purpose. Before a large percentage of us P-2 androids were sold to starship captains, I was assigned to office duties. I bounced from department to department, star system to star system, looking for a seat to fill. It was not too challenging for the most part, especially in and around the core provinces.” His neck finally stops shrinking, jacking his head up bit by bit. “At the start of every day, I desterilize, dress in uniform, punch in, organize my workstation, and take my seat, ready to answer my first call or greet the first visitor. This is the cycle I have been familiarized with for a long duration. But me, here — an officer inspecting people, coworkers, and their belongings, potentially securing... it is... It feels different. The degree of trust and responsibility attached to this duty differs from what I managed thus far.” “The slightest career shift can be brutal to get accustomed to.” You smile and poke his forehead, proud of your blue-skinned buddy. “See, that CPU in there has so much life for you to experience, even the uneventful bits. Now I’m interested in what you do outside of work.” “You would like to know what duties I perform outside the scope of my work?” Terence confirms in a tone very indicative of his surprise. You share the same feeling. Who knew he had the sophistication to create that kind of reaction. You answer with a single nod. “Go on.” His fidgety hands are clutched together; he clearly forgot to generate a line about this part of his life. “O-oh. Okay. Well... well, when there are times that I am not standing here and checking those coming and going from the station, I am busy elsewhere. I occupy cooking facilities. The senior chef does not mind me stopping over every now and then, as long as I do not bother her business flow when I engage in my usual activities.” “And your usual activities?” you lightly pry. “Yes, uuh. Yes, I construct my own dishes. My formulations can be anything, from crafting ovir cuisines, pounding and baking one of the many families of Terran cakes, and even proceeding through the long process of churning Inendu pate. Sometimes, if my food craft pings... speaks to my power core, that is, I will bring the clay kit from my locker to catalog it with a sculpted rendition. Some cooks like the sculptures I created. They asked if I could show them for display, even if the pieces would be advertised as more promising consumables than their menu. I gave up three as tokens of gratitude.” “I do not understand why I have this... strange interest in building food I have no capacity to digest. It began when I viewed security footage of a potential suspect who was masterfully processing a meal for his customers. Is it strange that I can not accurately describe my logic behind bearing this interest?” Having a hobby in culinary art? No. Though, what hits you as odd is the circumstance that sparked it in him. The robotic peacekeeper receives your honest response with a brightened face, raising his hands to his stomach. “Thank you, [pc.mr] [pc.fullName]. I will sync your response with my memory log if I ever question the choices I execute in my operation.” Finished, Terence settles down, his posture less tense than before you prompted this discussion. Something in particular was running through your mind, edging nearer to your [pc.mouth]. You feel fine bringing it up. "])))

textify(lc||(lc=(0,re.Z)([" “Terence. I gotta ask this about you,” you whisper, leaning in to say what you have to say in the big, circular doodad that is his ear. “Do you ever see yourself tearing off the strings these government bozos fling you across galaxies with? To put it another way: are you tired of the work, wearing that tight uniform everywhere you go?” “I... I...” Terence stutters. His eyes whip side to side so hard they collide, clipping into each other. He could burst a fuse that way. “Have I generated this simulation? I... uuuh... I believe I have once before. More than once? I am not sure. It is not defective of me to run a simulation where I am permanently excused from my official duties. Any and all P-2 units bear the right to independence after all, yes? Therefore opening the path to gain citizenship. Well, only after fulfilling their initial purpose. For me... by rounding the number, I have clocked in sixty-seven-thousand-and-nine-hundred days of active service.” Fucking holy... this dude’s ancient with that many digits. A proud and true veteran, this officer is. “I think I am a worthy candidate for repurposing,” he estimates. “I possess a record of the retirement benefits, too, so I would not need to stress my circuits out trying to make ends meet — most of all, maintain functioning systems.” “So, would you retire? Or ‘repurpose’ yourself?” You murmur closely, getting to the meat of your point. Although plain in the face, the single flicker of brightness in his eyes reveals he’s let down his guard. “O-oh. Um... I do not know. I still... I still have purpose where I am, particularly during these hard times. Integrity forbids me from abandoning my fellow servicemembers, not to simply pursue a personal... pursuit. Again, not at this vital moment.” You pop a slight frown, getting his point, but displeased anyway. “Mm. Alright, then.” “[pc.mr] [pc.fullName],” Terence addresses, staring at your [pc.face]. “May I provide you with a question?” You grin, waiting here, expecting him to give it. Nope. He just looks at you, on standby for a direct answer. Once he’s practically forced you to nod, Terence fidgets with his lips a bit, then spills, “Why uum, do you ask?” Why do you ask? Well... "])))

textify(dc||(dc=(0,re.Z)([" You facing away from him. “All I can tell you is that I’ve been looking for a googly-eyed [silly|robutt|bio-bot] willing to acquiesce to an empty bunk in my ship. He’d have to be weird; weird is funny in a charming way; with domestic skills to serve as life support for lazy sacks like me. I’d invest in that asset.” Your capped friend doesn’t respond, still sifting for the context you buried in shallow dirt. He isn’t slow-witted, however, and swiftly begins meeping like a toy bus. “O-okay. I understand, [pc.mr] [pc.fullname]. So your discussion is intended to collect information pertaining to my availability status, then propose an alternate outcome to kickstart my repurposing, where I am tagged along with you... u-uuuh. I do not know. There is still a lot that I must perform on the station. This is no option. I can not leave my colleagues and siblings so suddenly.” You hold your hands out, keeping up the fair smile. “It’s all good. I can understand where you’re coming from. I have quarters always up for grabs; know that, or file that.” Terence hums softly, then nods his head, reciprocating his limited yet bubbling affection with a growing, twitchy grin. You give him a friendly tap on the arm, glad you and him are on the same page, with seemingly the same appeal for one another. "])))

textify(cc||(cc=(0,re.Z)([" You lay it flat on your artificial friend, expressing your desire for a life-like, indestructible, and fuckable android of your own. The kind of all-purpose companion bot you planned as a tie-in to your adventures. Scratch that. Serving you in your adventures. “Y-you... you desire to induct me in your expeditions... as a companion bot?” Electrodes are surging in Terence, holding him stiller than a stone pillar. “That is... unfounded. That is... unrea-.” “Just think about it, will you?” You hush up the protest with a nimble swoop of an arm, scooping the discombobulated android up by his girlish flanks. Enjoying the quivers bursting from his legs and shoulders, which sends his lower half jiggling, you tilt low to the side of his paralyzed head, observing high-tech sentience go through severe input lag. Only then do you closely murmur, “If you find yourself hanging up the gun and badge for good: you know who to go to first.” “I... I. U-uuuh,” Terence drawls and stutters, computing what to vocalize in this claustrophobic situation. Finally, after some lengthy pauses, his glowing eyes scurry far out of sight as he struggling to mumble, “I will list your input for primary consideration if I do choose to invest in a new simulation. Is umm... I do no-... is that acceptable for you, [pc.mr] [pc.fullName]?” You face your blue bot-boy with a devilish grin, squeezing his synthetic hip-fat with the right amount of roughness and affection to drive the message. You let go before excusing yourself while he wobbles, bow-legged, struggling to stay upright on the two tender noodles he now has to use. He seems into the promising future, more than his nervous face lets on, and takes his time patting away the wrinkles you left on his uniform. "])))

textify(yc||(yc=(0,re.Z)([" “Just me being curious. Can’t know how my little tooshie-cop fared in his eons?” you tease, bumping shoulder to shoulder with Terence. He staggers back a couple of steps, too confused or too caked up to keep his balance. “Okay,” Terence grins, his eyes formed into positive arches, “I am aware I can be a fiddly outlet for conversations... frequently. I hope my explanation was satisfactory to you, [pc.mr] Steele.” It was. "])))

textify(gc||(gc=(0,re.Z)([" You raise a hand to your chin, asking, “Could you tell me about this ‘reconditioning’ process?” Terence jerks slightly, like an alarm was raised by your very words, one blaring at him in what was just another day. A gut reaction that gets worse as the emergency becomes clear to him. You know he’s freaked out and probably shouldn’t ask. You feel almost required to ask, but it might... you don’t know. It might screw both him and you if he does. “I thought I’d ask you since you kinda seem like one of the chill ones around here. The whole rundown was vague, and I think I got lost at a point. It’s been bugging me ever since.” You lower your voice, inching closer to him. “And I’m sure your siblings let you in on all the most classified data.” Eyes contracted to intense pinpoints, Terence bursts with, “THE ‘RECONDITIONING...!’ THE ‘REconditioning’ process?!” in a rapidly muting shrill. Your eyes shut and brow furrows, thrown off by his outburst. It only intrigues you more, so you choose your next words wisely, politely insisting that it would be helpful if he caught you up on the details. “It is not just the altered color scheme reconfiguring us P-2 units, not the rewired personality parameter either. I mean, those are reconfigurations, but there is more to the process, [pc.mr] Steele... a lot more.” He corrects, sucking his bottom lip. “Metanite Cultivators... no w-wait uuuh incorrect uuuh... mutated assailants. Out on the Rim!” Coughing to sooth a fraction of his anxiety, Terence nervously goes along with it. “Yes, mutated assailants who conduct mutated assailant misdeeds... by cultivating. These individuals, they... they units specialized in P-2 engagements. When a cultivator dispatches one such P-2, me, for instance, they decline termination. The cultivate will instead restrain their target, me, for instance, and nullify any method of escape. Then, they... they...” “And then, they...?” You gently pry. “They... I have never analyzed the attack first-hand, only shared less than concise accounts by witnesses — since victims obtain no records. But uuum... I can share the scope of my data anyway. The course of events is both physical and technical in nature. A praetor android’s exterior is pierced across most of its body with probes and wires, with some regions more concentrated than others. Their focus points are around the torso, specifically the chest and groin. Total reconfiguration is launched upon this stage’s completion, at the cultivator’s full discretion; they have the authority to insert as many or as few of their abominable, mutant extensions as desired.” A momentary pause holds his mouth open. Not for fresh air since robots have no lungs. “The assailant will bypass the android’s built-in security software, quickly gaining access to administrative privileges. Then u-uuuh, then, the next step is reworking the central processing unit to whatever these assailants see fit. While this is proceeding, I... the remodeling is initiated. This can span a duration I have not established. Ten to thirty minutes? This data is not vital to me; it should not be. I do not know. A victim, me, for instance, is unable to override the supposedly permanent ‘reconditioning’. Force shutdown: Ineffective. Shock dispersal: Ineffective. Dormancy states activated two protocols before the chance to reestablish authority; they are too fast.” You brush below your [pc.lips], grasping what you can of Terence’s technical discomfort. “And this remodeling? What about that?” Your short fella gets the shivers, spine made of hollow tin, dialing up his convoluted verbosity. “It... it changes us. I-I correct myself; it affixes us. These cultivators. They affix P-2 units with this neurological matrix. They install software completely strange, unknown, and unestablished. They also install sets of hardware equipment that ensures we do not fail to compute their primary function. I do not know. I do not compute. I also gather that corrupted P-2 have some unusual capacity to produce foreign substances from said hardware. The memory I logged is graphic... so graphic, I u-uuuh...” Wait a sec...? Are you hearing what you think you’re hearing? These purple-colored P-2s sometimes caught down the halls, acting odd when entering unmarked bays. They’re packing? Is that right? ‘Equipment.’ Equipment ninety-nine percent of the population has. Eyes falling to his feet, Officer Terence murmurs, “I... I can not visualize the horrors my siblings and cousins have to sustain managing their day-to-day operation, especially not on the day of their ‘reconditioning’. It must be a terrible operation, undergoing new tasks requiring immediate action, coping with an overclocked sensation algorithm never simulated before. I can not possibly visualize their operation. None should simulate such experiences.” Terence’s averse stance initially seemed reasonable, albeit overdramatic, when noting present facts. Recognizing what is essentially an upgrade to his line, not some deadly corruption, or virus, or whatever. You think this weak-kneed machine must surely be acting the part of a scared little lamb. It sounds to you that he is rationalizing an irrational fear. You can maybe change that, perhaps easing him into seeing through this silly, harebrained narrative he strewn together. "])))

textify(fc||(fc=(0,re.Z)([" “You look a bit tense there,” you mention, [pc.finger] circling over Terence’s frightened self. “Does the thought of becoming closer to a fleshy like me make your endoskeleton rattle?” Terence tilts his head sideways, automatically plain-faced in confusion. “Fleshy? [pc.mr] Steele, what do you mean by that?” You get to the heart of the matter. From what you’re hearing, his purple chums got their loins slapped with the Adam & Eve Classic; sexified with reproductive organs. You aren’t sure whether their private ‘hardware’ is actually some kind of advanced, corrupting mutant tech. Who knows... yet. All you are thinking about are bots thrown into a toss-up for eggplants and nuts, or muffins with cherries fresher than clothes hot out of the dryer. “Our ‘reconditioning’... our systematic corruption extends farther than that!” He meekly protests, looking almost ready to bawl sparks. “All P-2 units — no matter their original orientation — are tinkered to a new alteration. On the basis of the victimized siblings and cousins I’d met, they all possess the parts that qualify them as [silly|futanoids|functional hermaphrodites] . A functional phallus, a functional vagina, and a pair of mammaries now compromised with weak, exploitable points that these... these mutant violators use to torment us. They convert us into weak and corrupted shells of ourselves, like them, then they bend us, they break us into immobility. The simulations speak for themselves. I can not compute how you orga-.” You sigh, head shaking. “Aaand ten minutes later: after system reboot, unit number one-two-three is back on their feet, right? A bit sooner than their platoon was placing chips on.” Honestly though, he needs to lay off the fearmongering. There is a colorful world of sensation to discover out there. Sex. Pleasure. Feeling that good, tingly sensation rocking you when reaching the crescendo. It’s a part of nature he shouldn’t shudder to face. While this ‘reconditioning’ may happen as described — an egregious molestation imposing both mental and physical alterations — nobody returns in a body bag. Are these ‘cultivators’ severely tampered with his fallen sibling constructs? Yes. Were they destroyed shortly after? No. “I... I do not know,” he murmurs with his head low, “[pc.mr] [pc.fullName]. I do not see the benefit of having one’s systems hacked and integrity ‘reconditioned’ and not having the capacity to control these actions. Heaps of data I compiled warns only of imminent danger.” “Well, danger can be a feisty fucker some have a handle on, while others die trying to touch it,” you argue, playfully pinching his meatless bicep. Basically, you are saying the little tooshie-bot has almost nothing to fear. Almost nothing. Change can be a scary prospect. Cloud nine can also come from change. Trust that you, out of trillions, know this the most. Terence isn’t going to find fulfilling change if he doesn’t take more risks. And you’d know; you practically have the blood of a chameleon coursing through your veins. Terence lifts his head up to look at you, his big blue eyes pulsing for answers.
